content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
Sacramento. CA 958)2-0807
(9)6) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
July 29, 1999
E. Ken Tokutomi
Tokutomi & Caruthers
1010 Racquet Club Drive, Suite 105
Auburn, California 95603
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-194
Dear Mr. Tokutomi:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be
construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place. Further, this letter
is based on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Commission does not act as the
finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) As will be explained
below, because we do not have sufficient material facts upon which to render a formal analysis,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance under the Act. Informal assistance
does not provide the immunity conferred by formal advice rendered under Section 83114 and
Regulation 18329 (copies enclosed).
QUESTIONS
1. May Mr. Jim Johnston distribute campaign information in the form of cards, much
like baseball cards, with preprinted numbers that will then entitle the recipient of the card to
participate in a cash drawing?
2. May the Committee to Elect Jim Johnston (“the committee”) pay for the printing of
the cards with campaign funds and may the committee use campaign funds to pay for the cash
prize?
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. 1-99-194
Page 2
FACTS
You are currently the treasurer for the Committee to Elect Jim Johnston, 1St Senate
District, and have been authorized by Mr. Johnston to seek this advice on his behalf.
Mr. Johnston would like to distribute campaign information cards with preprinted
numbers that would entitle the recipient of the cards and numbers to participate in a cash drawing
for $500 to be conducted during the latter part of the campaign. Mr. Johnston’s campaign
committee would like to use its campaign funds to pay for the printing of the cards and to fund
the cash drawing.
ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSIONS
1. May Mr. Jim Johnston distribute campaign information in theform ofcards, much
like baseball cards, with preprinted numbers that will then entitle the reczient ofthe card to
participate in a cash drawing?
Nothing in the Act prevents Mr. Johnston or his campaign committee from
communicating with the voters regarding his desire to become an elected official. However, this
office may only offer advice with respect to the Act. We cannot comment as to whether any
other provision of California or federal law would prohibit the “cards for cash drawing”
communication you have described. You may wish to contact the Secretary of State and the
Attorney General’s offices concerning this “game of chance” aspect of the cards.
2. May the Committee to Elect Jim Johnston (“the committee”) payfor the printing of
the cards with campaignfunds and may the committee use campaignfunds to payfor the cash
prize?
Use ofcampaignfunds to print cards.
Section 89512 allows a candidate to expend campaign finds for purposes of seeking
office as long as the expenditure is “reasonably related to a political purpose.” We assume that
the distribution of the cards has the possibility of enhancing Mr. Johnston’s chances ofbecoming
elected. Therefore, the expenditure of campaign funds to print the cards is reasonably related to
a political purpose.
Use ofcampaign funds to payfor the cash prize to be awarded at the drawing
You have not specifically described the process by which a person will “win” the cash
prize at the drawing. We assume, however, that the winner will be selected exclusively on the
basis ofhis or her card number and that no other action on that person’s partwill be required. If


File No. 1-99-194
Page 3
our assumption is correct, the cash prize would constitute a gift to that person since he or she will
not be providing consideration in return for the award.
Pursuant to Section 89513(0(1), campaign funds may not be used to make personal gifts
unless the gift is “directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.” You have
not provided us with sufficient facts upon which we can determine if the award of the cash prize
is directly related to one of the above-enumerated purposes. Therefore, we cannot advise you
that the use ofthe campaign funds for the cash prize would comply with the requirements of the
Act. Additionally, as stated above, we are concerned that this conduct may not be allowable by
other provisions of California or federal law even ifyou could establish that the award of the
cash prize would have a direct relationship to, for instance, the political purpose of enhancing
Mr. Johnston’s ability to be elected. We suggest you consult the previous noted references
andlor an attorney for further advice on this particular issue.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lisa L. Ditora
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
